deck: Dimagi Chief of Staff Gillian Javetski joins Jonathan Jackson to unpack why global health and development reward out-collaborating over out-competing, and what separates good collaboration from bad.
seo_description: Gillian Javetski and Jonathan Jackson on why out-collaborating beats out-competing in global development, and what makes a collaboration good or bad.
og_description: Why out-collaborating beats out-competing in global development, and how Dimagi picks its partnerships.
about: Jonathan Jackson and Gillian Javetski, Dimagi's Chief of Staff, sit down with Amie Vaccaro to discuss why making progress on complex international development and global health challenges depends on collaboration rather than competition. With resources scarce and projects involving many stakeholders, governments, funders, and implementing partners, they argue your core skill becomes how well you collaborate, not how well you compete.

Drawing on decades of Dimagi experience, including the COVID vaccine response work with Ona and Medic and the early Open Rosa and JavaRosa open source communities, the conversation unpacks what distinguishes good collaboration from bad. The healthiest collaborations, they argue, are selfish and built around a shared problem both organizations genuinely need to solve, and the strongest ones level up from the transactional task to the higher level outcome the public sector and patients actually want.

Gillian and Jonathan also confront a paradox: despite a deeply collaborative culture, people have called Dimagi uncollaborative. They draw a line between knowledge sharing and true collaboration toward a shared goal, explain why Dimagi declines meetings that exist only for status updates, and reflect on when to re-evaluate or end a collaboration that has run its course.

pull_quote.text: Collaboration for the sake of collaboration is a terrible idea. It never works. So the healthiest collaborations are selfish. Your organization was going to try to solve this problem anyways, and you are selfishly coming into the collaboration, recognizing you have a big thing you want to gain from it.

bullets: Out-collaborating vs out-competing: why scarce resources and complex projects make collaboration, not competition, the core skill in global development
Good vs bad collaboration: the difference between advocating for shared change and just showing up to a working group to give status updates
The selfish collaboration: why the healthiest partnerships start from a problem your organization genuinely needs to solve
Leveling up: stepping above the transactional task to the higher level outcome the public sector and patients really want
Collaboration in action: the COVID vaccine response with Ona and Medic, and the Open Rosa and JavaRosa communities that had a healthy beginning, middle, and end
Why some called Dimagi uncollaborative: knowledge sharing versus working toward a shared goal, and the meetings worth declining
Knowing when to stop: spotting cultural misalignment, auditing long running collaborations, and recognizing that a failed one is not personal
